review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: Module-level mutable Map cache in discover-sources.ts creates shared process state

evidence: Line 18: `const catalogCache = new Map<string, Source[]>()` — module-level mutable singleton
Cache is keyed by path but shared across all sessions in the same process — a session with a custom sourceCatalogPath could observe stale data if the catalog file changes mid-process
The toolkit architecture intends session-scoped isolation (DemosSession is session-scoped), but this cache is process-scoped
detail.suggestion: This is low-priority given the catalog is static per deployment. If the concern matters, document the cache-invalidation assumption in a comment (e.g., '// catalog is assumed static per process — restart to pick up catalog changes'). Alternatively, move the cache into DemosSession if per-session catalog isolation becomes needed.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: loadCatalog() calls JSON.parse without local try/catch, relying on outer function's catch in different scope

evidence: discover-sources.ts line 80: `const catalog = JSON.parse(raw)` — no try/catch in loadCatalog()
The outer try/catch is in discoverSources() (line 43), a different function that calls loadCatalog(). This works but the error handling is owned by the caller, not the function that parses
If loadCatalog is called from another context in the future, the JSON.parse throw would propagate unhandled
Flagged by the security detector as 'JSON.parse() without try/catch - may throw on malformed input'
detail.suggestion: Add a local try/catch inside loadCatalog() around the JSON.parse, throwing a descriptive error: `try { catalog = JSON.parse(raw); } catch { throw new Error(`Invalid JSON in catalog at ${catalogPath}`); }`. This makes the error contract explicit at the parse site.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: tip-spend-cap and pay-spend-cap check policy bounds before validating isFinite — relies on NaN comparison behavior

evidence: tip-spend-cap.ts line 36: `if (amount > policy.maxPerTip)` checked before line 44: `if (!Number.isFinite(amount) || amount <= 0)`. NaN > 10 is false, so NaN would pass the first check and be caught by the second — but this depends on implicit NaN arithmetic.
pay-spend-cap.ts line 34: `if (amount > policy.maxPerCall)` checked before line 42: `if (!Number.isFinite(amount) || amount <= 0)`. Same pattern.

detail.suggestion: Move the `isFinite(amount) && amount > 0` validation to be the FIRST check in both functions, before any policy comparison. This makes validation order self-documenting and removes the implicit NaN-comparison dependency.

review::.::holistic::type_safety::backof.summary: backoff.ts uses non-null assertions (!) on lastResult and lastResult.error after loop — TypeScript cannot verify safety

The retry loop always runs attempt=0, so lastResult IS always set, but TypeScript cannot prove it from the type alone
